sourceMovie.title: My Bloody Valentine
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit My Bloody Valentine for the same reason it broke through in 2009: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a thriller rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Patrick Lussier keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The horror and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Hills Have Eyes
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If My Bloody Valentine clicked for you, The Hills Have Eyes (2006) is a strong next stop: both trade in horror and thriller, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Mirrors
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Mirrors (2008) comes earlier and helps explain the emotional blueprint that My Bloody Valentine later built on. Watch it as a predecessor to see where those themes and character tensions started.

faq.question: What should I watch after My Bloody Valentine?
faq.answer: Try The Hills Have Eyes, Wrong Turn, Terrifier 2—each shares DNA with My Bloody Valentine while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is My Bloody Valentine?
faq.answer: My Bloody Valentine (2009) is best described as Horror, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is My Bloody Valentine worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 5.6/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this horror lane.
